Up to ten clones of each cell line were tested for inflammasome activation and the level of ASC expression was assessed by western blotting and a representative clone was selected for further analysis.
   131→
   132→For transduction of primary bone marrow cells, retroviral particles were generated in Phoenix-Eco packaging cells and used to transduce WT C57BL/6 bone marrow cells after 48 and 72 h of culture in medium with 10% 3T3-MCSF supernatant. Inflammasome stimulation was performed 4 days after the first transduction24.
   133→
   134→Immortalized BMDMs (seeded at 250 000 cells per ml in 96-well plates) were primed for 4 h with 100 ng ml−1 LPS O55:B5 (Invivogen). The NLRP3 inflammasome was triggered by addition of 5 mM extracellular ATP (Sigma-Aldrich) for 60 min. The AIM2 inflammasome was triggered by transfection of 1 μg ml−1 of the synthetic DNA analogue poly(dA:dT) (Invivogen) using Lipofectamine 2000 (Invitrogen), according to the manufacturer's protocol, in OptiMEM (Gibco) for 3 h. The NLRC4 inflammasome was triggered by infection of the cells with S. enterica serovar Typhimurium SL1344 at a multiplicity of infection of 10. The infection was synchronized by centrifugation and continued for 60 min. The pyrin inflammasome was triggered by addition of 1 μg ml−1 (final concentration) of C. difficile toxin B (CdtB, Enzo Biotech) for 2.5 h.
   135→
   136→IL-1β release was measured by ELISA (eBiosciences). Cell death was quantified by measuring LDH release using the LDH Cytotoxicity detection kit (TaKaRa Clontech). To normalize for spontaneous cell lysis, the percentage of cell death was calculated as follows: [(LDH sample)−(LDH negative control)]/[(LDH positive control)−(LDH negative control)] × 100.
   137→
   138→Cells were seeded on coverslips (150 000 cells per coverslip) and treated as described above with the addition of 25 μM Z-VAD-fmk (Bachem) to prevent detachment of pyroptotic cells and therefore loss of cells with ASC specks. Coverslips were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA) (15 min, 37 °C, Alfa Aesar) and washed with PBS. Nuclei were stained with Hoechst 33342 (Life Technologies) for 10 min and the slides mounted using Vectashield (Vector Laboratories). For quantifications of ASC aggregates (specks or filaments), ten random regions of interest were imaged at × 20 magnification (Leica DMI3000B inverted fluorescence microscope, HCX PL FLUOTAR objective, Leica DFC3000G camera and LAS AF Version 3 software) and the number of ASC aggregates and cells were counted. For representative images, the slides were imaged at × 63 magnification (Leica point scanning confocal ‘SP8', HC PL APO CS2 × 63 objective, Leica AF software version 3). For measurements of speck sizes, random regions of interest were images at × 63 magnification (Leica point scanning confocal ‘SP8') and the largest diameters of individual specks were measured using Fiji63.
   139→
   140→For western blotting of supernatant and lysate samples, cells were seeded at 106 cells per well in six-well plates and treated as described above. Supernatants were precipitated with 10% trichloracetic acid, precipitates washed with acetone and resuspended in 40 μl 1 × SDS–PAGE sample buffer and boiled at 95 °C for 10 min. Cells were lysed with 200 μl 1 × RIPA (50 mM Tris-HCl pH 7.5, 150 mM NaCl, 0.1% SDS, 0.5% sodium deoxycholate, 1% NP-40, 1 × protease inhibitor cocktail (Roche)) for 30 min on ice. Lysates were re-suspended in 5 × SDS–PAGE sample buffer and boiled at 95 °C for 10 min64. For combined supernatant and lysates, samples were prepared as above, but supernatant precipitates were resuspended in lysate samples. Samples were run on 14% (supernatants or lysates) or 12% (combined SN+lysates) acrylamide gels (1 h, 170 V, 40 mA per gel), transferred to polyvinylidene difluoride membranes (1 h, 100 V constant), blocked in 5% milk in Tris-buffered saline+Tween-20 (TBS-T) and incubated with primary antibodies in 5% BSA–TBS-T for 16 h at 4 °C or 2 h at room temperature with agitation. Secondary antibodies were diluted 1:5,000 in 5% milk–TBS-T and incubated for 1 h at room temperature. The membranes were developed using either LumiGLO (KPL) or LumiGLO Reserve (KPL). The following antibodies and dilutions were used: rat anti-Caspase-1 p20 (Genentech, 1:1,000; Supplementary Figs 1b,d and 2c,d) or mouse anti-Caspase 1 p20 (1:4,000, AG20B-0042, AdipoGen; Fig. 6a and Supplementary Fig. 8a), rat anti-ASC (Genentech, 1:2,000; Fig. 5a and Supplementary Fig. 2a,h) or rabbit anti-ASC (1:1,000, AG25B-006, Adipogen, all other figures), rabbit anti-IL-18 (5180R-100, Biovision, 1:1,000), goat anti-IL-1β (AF-401-NA, R&D, 1:1,000), mouse anti-GFP (632381, Clontech, 1:1,000), mouse anti-mCherry (ab125096, Abcam, 1:1,000), mouse anti-V5 (R960-25, Invitrogen, 1:1,000), rabbit anti-HMGB1 (GTX-101277, Genetex, 1:1,000), mouse anti-gasdermin-D (GSDMDC1 (A-7), Santa Cruz Biotechnology, 1:1,000; Supplementary Fig. 7), rabbit anti-gasdermin-D (1:2,000, G7422, Sigma, all other figures) and mouse anti-β-actin (A1987, Sigma, 1:1,000).
   141→
   142→Co-immunoprecipitation was done according to a modified protocol published previously49. For assessment of ASC/AIM2 interactions, 3 × 800,000 HEK293T cells were transfected with 2 μg per well AIM2-V5 and ASC-mCherry (ASCFL, ASCK21A, ASCY59A or ASCE80R) encoding plasmids at a ratio of 1:4 using linear polyethylenimine (PEI, Polysciences) at a ratio DNA:PEI of 1:4 (refs 65, 66). Twenty-four hours after transfection, the cells were washed twice with ice-cold 1 × PBS and lysed using HEK lysis buffer (20 mM HEPES pH 7.4, 10 mM KCl, 1 mM EDTA, 0.1 mM phenylmethylsulfonyl fluoride, 1 mM Na3VO4, 5 mM NaF, 0.5 % Nonidet P-40 and 1 × protease inhibitor cocktail (Roche)). Lysates were sonicated for 5 × 7 s before removing debris and non-lysed cells by centrifugation (10,000 g, 15 min, 4 °C). The samples were incubated with 1 μg mouse anti-V5 antibody (R960-25, Invitrogen) or a control antibody (mouse anti-GFP, 632381, Clontech) with agitation for 16 h at 4 °C. For assessment of ASCCARD/ASCCARD interactions, 3 × 800 000 HEK293T cells were transfected with 1 μg per well ASC-mCherry (ASCFL, ASCPYD, ASCD130R or ASCD134R), 1 μg per well ASCCARD-GFP and 0.2 μg AIM2-V5, to initiate ASC/ASC interactions using linear PEI. Forty hours after transfection, the cells were washed, lysed and sonicated as described above. The samples were then incubated with 1 μg mouse anti-mCherry antibody (ab125096, Abcam) or a control antibody (mouse anti-HA, MMS-101R-200, Covance). The samples were then incubated with agitation for 2 h at 4 °C with 25 μl Pierce Protein A Plus Agarose bead slurry (22810, Thermo Scientific). Beads were washed three times with lysis buffer (centrifugation 1 min 1,000 g, 4 °C), resuspended in 2 × SDS–PAGE sample buffer (30 μl) and boiled at 95 °C, before analysing the eluted proteins by western blotting as described above.
   143→
   144→Two guide RNAs52 targeting exon 4 of Casp1 (5′-gagggcaagacgtgtacgag-3′ and 5′-cgagtggttgtattcattat-3′) and one guide RNA targeting exon 2 of Gsdmd (5′-ggtcaagaatgtgatcaagg-3′) were cloned into lentiCRISPRv2 harbouring a puromycin resistance cassette (Addgene53). These constructs were transfected into HEK293T cells using PEI (as described above) together with the lentiviral packaging vector PsPax2 (Addgene) and the lentviral envelope vector VSV-G (Addgene). Sixteen hours after transfection, medium was exchanged with macrophage medium and incubated at 37 °C for 2 days to produce lentiviral particles. The lentiviral particles were used to transfect the immortalized macrophage cell lines (800,000 cells per well in 6-well plates) using polybrene (Merck) to favour virus attachment. Two days after viral transduction, the macrophages were expanded. Attached macrophages were then treated with 10 μg ml−1 puromycin (Gibco) for 6 days to select for successful lentiviral transduction. After puromycin selection, the cells were tested for successful knockout by western blotting.
   145→
   146→The ASCPYD (residues 1–91) was cloned with a C-terminal His6 tag into the pET28a vector under the control of a T7 promoter. Protein expression was induced by isopropyl-β-D-thiogalactopyranosid addition in BL21(DE3) Escherichia coli at an OD600 of 0.8 for 4 h at 37 °C. Bacteria were harvested by centrifugation and resuspended in 50 mM phosphate buffer pH 7.5, 300 mM NaCl, with Complete protease inhibitor (Roche). Resuspended bacteria were incubated for 1 h at room temperature with DNase I, sonicated on ice and centrifuged at 20,000 g at 4 °C for 30 min. The pellet, including ASCPYD-containing inclusion bodies, was solubilized in 50 mM phosphate buffer pH 7.5, 300 mM NaCl, 6 M guanidinium hydrochloride and centrifuged at 20,000 g at 4 °C for 30 min. The supernatant was incubated for 2 h at room temperature with pre-equilibrated Ni-NTA affinity resin (Thermo Scientific) and then passed through a plastic body column for gravity flow purification. The column was washed with 20 column volumes of solubilization buffer containing 20 mM imidazole and eluted with 3 column volumes of solubilization buffer with 500 mM imidazole. The pH of the elution fraction was decreased to 3.8 and dialysed against 50 mM glycine buffer pH 3.8, 150 mM NaCl. The protein was further purified on a pre-equilibrated Superdex 75 gel filtration column (GE Healthcare). This gel-filtration step removed traces of pre-existing aggregates and yielded highly pure, monomeric soluble form of ASCPYD. Samples were either used immediately or stored after flash-freezing in small aliquots in liquid N2.
   147→
   148→Immediately before the experiments, samples of monomeric soluble ASCPYD were centrifuged at 20,000 g at 4 °C for 30 min and filtered with 0.1 μM filter (Millipore). The protein concentration was adjusted to 25 μM by dilution from a higher-concentrated stock solution. Filament formation was triggered by rapid dilution to neutral pH. Thereby, 70 μl of monomeric ASCPYD was mixed with 0.45 μl of 2.75 M NaOH solution to a reach the pH of 7.5. The solution was mixed at room temperature by careful pipetting, to avoid introduction of air bubbles, and immediately transferred to a quartz cuvette with 1 cm path length. Between runs, cuvettes were carefully cleaned with 1 M Hellmanex solution (Sigma-Aldrich) to avoid cross-seeding effects between sequential measurements. Filament growth was monitored by dynamic light scattering with a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS series instrument. The laser focal spot was positioned in the middle of the cuvette and maintained fixed for all the measurements. To maximize the intensity of the scattered light, the minimal attenuation level was used. Data were acquired in 60 s intervals by averaging three runs of 20 s, until a total time of 350 min. Afterwards, the protein solution was blotted on EM grids, negatively stained and imaged with transmission electron microscopy to visualize filament formation.
   149→
   150→Filament growth was modelled assuming pseudo-first-order kinetics where the filament propagation step occurs by the addition of monomers to the initial growth centre. The time-dependent growth signal I was fitted independently for each measurement by a single exponential function,
   151→
   152→
   153→
   154→where I∞ corresponds to the signal at time t and at infinitive time, respectively, and kF is the first-order rate constant. Fits were done with nonlinear least-square minimization.
   155→
   156→The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon request.
   157→
   158→How to cite this article: Dick, M. S. et al. ASC filament formation serves as a signal amplification mechanism for inflammasomes. Nat. Commun. 7:11929 doi: 10.1038/ncomms11929 (2016).
   159→
   160→Supplementary Figures 1-9, Supplementary Table 1 and Supplementary References.

input.content:
# Research: Common Constituents of NLR (Nucleotide-Binding Leucine-Rich Repeat Receptor) Complexes

## Summary

NLR (nucleotide-binding leucine-rich repeat) receptors are intracellular immune receptors found in both plants and animals that recognize pathogen-derived molecules and danger signals. Upon activation, they form large oligomeric signaling complexes. While the core NLR proteins share common domain architectures, the accessory proteins that physically associate with them differ significantly between plant resistosomes and animal inflammasomes.

---

## PLANT NLR COMPLEX CONSTITUENTS

### 1. CC-NLR Resistosomes (e.g., ZAR1)

CC-NLRs (coiled-coil NLRs) form pentameric resistosome complexes that function directly as calcium-permeable cation channels at the plasma membrane.

#### Core constituents:
- **Sensor NLR protein** (e.g., ZAR1) - forms the pentameric resistosome
- **RLCK (Receptor-Like Cytoplasmic Kinase) pseudokinases** - associates with some CC-NLRs as guardees (e.g., PBL2, RKS1 with ZAR1)

### 2. Helper NLR Resistosomes (NRC, NRG1, ADR1)

Helper NLRs function downstream of sensor NLRs and also form oligomeric complexes that act as calcium channels.

#### Core constituents:
- **Helper NLR protein** (NRG1 or ADR1 family members, or NRC proteins)
- **Hexameric or pentameric oligomer** of helper NLR forms the resistosome

### 3. TIR-NLR (TNL) Signaling Complexes

TNLs form tetrameric resistosomes with NADase enzymatic activity. They signal through lipase-like adaptor proteins.

#### Core constituents:
- **TIR-NLR protein** - forms tetrameric resistosome with NADase activity
- **EDS1 (Enhanced Disease Susceptibility 1)** - lipase-like signaling adaptor
- **PAD4 (Phytoalexin Deficient 4)** - EDS1 heterodimer partner, lipase-like protein
- **SAG101 (Senescence-Associated Gene 101)** - EDS1 heterodimer partner, lipase-like protein
- EDS1-PAD4 heterodimer functions with ADR1 helper NLRs
- EDS1-SAG101 heterodimer functions with NRG1 helper NLRs

---

## ANIMAL NLR COMPLEX CONSTITUENTS (INFLAMMASOMES)

### 1. NLRP3 Inflammasome

The most extensively studied inflammasome, activated by diverse stimuli.

#### Core constituents:
- **NLRP3 sensor** - NLR protein that oligomerizes upon activation
- **NEK7 (NIMA-related kinase 7)** - bridges adjacent NLRP3 subunits; essential for NLRP3 oligomerization (kinase activity dispensable)
- **ASC (Apoptosis-associated Speck-like protein containing a CARD)** - adaptor protein; contains PYD and CARD domains; forms filaments
- **Caspase-1 (pro-form)** - effector cysteine protease; recruited via CARD-CARD interactions

### 2. NAIP/NLRC4 Inflammasome

Activated by bacterial flagellin and type III secretion system components.

#### Core constituents:
- **NAIP (NLR family Apoptosis Inhibitory Protein)** - sensor that directly binds bacterial ligands
  - NAIP5/NAIP6 recognize flagellin (mouse)
  - NAIP1/NAIP2 recognize T3SS needle/rod proteins (mouse)
  - Human NAIP recognizes T3SS needle protein
- **NLRC4** - adaptor NLR; contains CARD; oligomerizes with NAIP
- **ASC** - adaptor protein (enhances signaling but not essential for pyroptosis)
- **Caspase-1** - effector protease; can be recruited directly by NLRC4 CARD or via ASC

### 3. Other Inflammasomes (NLRP1, AIM2, Pyrin)

- **ASC** - required adaptor for PYD-containing sensors (AIM2, NLRP3, Pyrin)
- **Caspase-1** - effector for all canonical inflammasomes
- NLRP1 and NLRC4 can recruit caspase-1 directly via their CARD domains

### Downstream effectors (physically associated via caspase-1 cleavage):
- **Gasdermin D** - cleaved by caspase-1; N-terminal fragment forms membrane pores causing pyroptosis

---

## COMMON/GENERAL FEATURES ACROSS KINGDOMS

1. **Oligomerization** - Both plant resistosomes and animal inflammasomes form oligomeric structures upon activation
2. **Calcium signaling** - Plant resistosomes function as Ca2+ channels; animal inflammasomes also trigger Ca2+ signaling
3. **Adaptor proteins** - Both systems use adaptor proteins (ASC in animals; EDS1/PAD4/SAG101 in plants for TNLs)
4. **Helper/sensor relationships** - Both systems can involve sensor-helper NLR relationships

---

## VALIDATED REFERENCES

### PMID:37602488 "NLR immune receptors: structure and function in plant disease resistance."

Förderer A, Kourelis J. Biochem Soc Trans. 2023 Aug 31;51(4):1473-1483.

Review covering plant NLR domain architecture and resistosome formation.

* SUPPORT: PMID:37602488 "Nucleotide-binding and leucine-rich repeat receptors (NLRs) are a diverse family of intracellular immune receptors that play crucial roles in recognizing and responding to pathogen invasion in plants."
    - NLRs are intracellular immune receptors in plants
    - Reviews N-terminal executioner domains, NOD module, and LRR domain

---

### PMID:33984278 "The ZAR1 resistosome is a calcium-permeable channel triggering plant immune signaling."

Bi G, Su M, Li N, et al. Cell. 2021 Jun 24;184(13):3528-3541.e12.

Landmark paper demonstrating ZAR1 resistosome functions as a calcium channel.

* SUPPORT: PMID:33984278 "Upon activation, the Arabidopsis NLR protein ZAR1 forms a pentameric resistosome in vitro and triggers immune responses and cell death in plants."
    - ZAR1 forms pentameric resistosome
    - Functions as calcium-permeable cation channel
    - Glu11 residue essential for channel activity

---

### PMID:34140391 "Plant 'helper' immune receptors are Ca(2+)-permeable nonselective cation channels."

Jacob P, Kim NH, Wu F, et al. Science. 2021 Jul 23;373(6553):420-425.

Demonstrates helper NLRs (NRG1, ADR1) function as calcium channels.

* SUPPORT: PMID:34140391 "Active NRG1.1 oligomerized, was enriched in plasma membrane puncta, and conferred cytoplasmic calcium ion (Ca2+) influx in plant and human cells."
    - NRG1.1 and ADR1 are helper NLRs
    - Both form Ca2+-permeable cation channels
    - Conserved negatively charged N-terminal residues required for Ca2+ influx

---

### PMID:31311833 "A Coevolved EDS1-SAG101-NRG1 Module Mediates Cell Death Signaling by TIR-Domain Immune Receptors."

Lapin D, Kovacova V, Sun X, et al. Plant Cell. 2019 Oct;31(10):2430-2455.

Establishes the EDS1-SAG101-NRG1 signaling module in TNL immunity.

* SUPPORT: PMID:31311833 "Toll-interleukin 1 receptor domain NLRs (TNLs) converge on the ENHANCED DISEASE SUSCEPTIBILITY1 (EDS1) family of lipase-like proteins for all resistance outputs."
    - EDS1-SAG101-NRG1 is a coevolved cell death-signaling module
    - EDS1-PAD4 and EDS1-SAG101 are distinct signaling branches
    - EDS1 family proteins are lipase-like adaptors

---

### PMID:25621709 "The NAIP/NLRC4 inflammasomes."

Vance RE. Curr Opin Immunol. 2015 Feb;32:84-9.

Review of NAIP/NLRC4 inflammasome structure and function.

* SUPPORT: PMID:25621709 "NAIP proteins function as specific cytosolic receptors for a variety of bacterial protein ligands. Mouse NAIP5 recognizes flagellin, the primary protein constituent of the bacterial flagellum. Mouse NAIP1 and NAIP2 detect needle and inner rod proteins, respectively, which are key structural components of a bacterial secretion system called the type III secretion system (T3SS)."
    - NAIPs are sensors for bacterial ligands
    - NLRC4 is the adaptor that recruits caspase-1
    - NLRC4 contains a CARD domain for direct caspase-1 recruitment

* SUPPORT: PMID:25621709 "NAIPs and NLRC4 are homologous to each other (~20% amino acid identity) and are members of the functionally diverse NLR superfamily"
    - NAIP and NLRC4 co-oligomerize to form inflammasome
    - ASC not strictly required but enhances signaling

---

### PMID:31189953 "Structural mechanism for NEK7-licensed activation of NLRP3 inflammasome."

Sharif H, Wang L, Wang WL, et al. Nature. 2019 Jun;570(7761):338-343.

Cryo-EM structure of NLRP3-NEK7 complex revealing NEK7's bridging role.

* SUPPORT: PMID:31189953 "The mitotic kinase NEK7 licenses the assembly and activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome in interphase. Here we report a cryo-electron microscopy structure of inactive human NLRP3 in complex with NEK7, at a resolution of 3.8 Å."
    - NEK7 bridges adjacent NLRP3 subunits
    - NEK7 C-terminal lobe interacts with NLRP3 LRR and NACHT domains
    - Bipartite interactions mediate NLRP3 activation

* SUPPORT: PMID:31189953 "Modelling of an active NLRP3-NEK7 conformation based on the NLRC4 inflammasome predicts an additional contact between an NLRP3-bound NEK7 and a neighbouring NLRP3."
    - NEK7 is essential for NLRP3 oligomerization
    - NEK7 bridges gap between adjacent NLRP3 subunits in the inflammasome ring

---

### PMID:26814970 "NEK7 is an essential mediator of NLRP3 activation downstream of potassium efflux."

He Y, Zeng MY, Yang D, et al. Nature. 2016 Feb 18;530(7590):354-7.

Identification of NEK7 as essential NLRP3 inflammasome component.

* SUPPORT: PMID:26814970 "Here we report the identification of NEK7, a member of the family of mammalian NIMA-related kinases (NEK proteins), as an NLRP3-binding protein that acts downstream of potassium efflux to regulate NLRP3 oligomerization and activation."
    - NEK7 is essential for NLRP3 inflammasome activation
    - Catalytic activity of NEK7 is dispensable
    - NEK7 mediates NLRP3 oligomerization

---

### PMID:27329339 "ASC filament formation serves as a signal amplification mechanism for inflammasomes."

Dick MS, Sborgi L, Rühl S, et al. Nat Commun. 2016 Jun 22;7:11929.

Mechanistic study of ASC speck formation and its role in inflammasome signaling.

* SUPPORT: PMID:27329339 "A hallmark of inflammasome activation is the ASC speck, a micrometre-sized structure formed by the inflammasome adaptor protein ASC (apoptosis-associated speck-like protein containing a CARD), which consists of a pyrin domain (PYD) and a caspase recruitment domain (CARD)."
    - ASC is the central inflammasome adaptor protein
    - ASC contains PYD and CARD domains
    - ASC forms filaments via PYD-PYD interactions
    - ASC CARD cross-links filaments into speck structure
    - ASC speck serves as signal amplification mechanism

* SUPPORT: PMID:27329339 "Here we show that assembly of the ASC speck involves oligomerization of ASC(PYD) into filaments and cross-linking of these filaments by ASC(CARD)."
    - ASC PYD forms the filament backbone
    - ASC CARD mediates filament cross-linking and caspase-1 recruitment

---

### PMID:27383986 "Inflammasome-activated gasdermin D causes pyroptosis by forming membrane pores."

Liu X, Zhang Z, Ruan J, et al. Nature. 2016 Jul 7;535(7610):153-8.

Discovery that gasdermin D forms membrane pores downstream of inflammasome activation.

* SUPPORT: PMID:27383986 "Inflammatory caspases (caspases 1, 4, 5 and 11) are activated in response to microbial infection and danger signals. When activated, they cleave mouse and human gasdermin D (GSDMD) after Asp276 and Asp275, respectively, to generate an N-terminal cleavage product (GSDMD-NT) that triggers inflammatory death (pyroptosis)"
    - Gasdermin D is cleaved by caspase-1
    - N-terminal fragment forms membrane pores
    - This is downstream effector, not a core complex constituent

---

## SUMMARY TABLE: Core Physical Constituents of NLR Complexes

| Kingdom | Complex Type | Core Physical Constituents |
|---------|--------------|---------------------------|
| Plants | CC-NLR resistosome | Sensor NLR (pentamer), associated guardee/decoy proteins |
| Plants | Helper NLR resistosome | Helper NLR (NRG1/ADR1/NRC; pentamer or hexamer) |
| Plants | TNL resistosome + signaling | TIR-NLR (tetramer), EDS1, PAD4 or SAG101 |
| Animals | NLRP3 inflammasome | NLRP3, NEK7, ASC, pro-caspase-1 |
| Animals | NAIP/NLRC4 inflammasome | NAIP, NLRC4, (ASC optional), pro-caspase-1 |
| Animals | AIM2/Pyrin inflammasome | Sensor (AIM2/Pyrin), ASC, pro-caspase-1 |

---

## Notes for GO Ontology Definition

For an NLR complex definition, the following proteins should be considered as common constituents:

**In plants:**
- The NLR protein itself (sensor or helper)
- For TNL signaling: EDS1 family lipase-like proteins (EDS1, PAD4, SAG101)
- Helper NLRs (NRG1, ADR1, NRC family) for sensor NLRs that require them

**In animals:**
- The NLR sensor protein
- ASC (apoptosis-associated speck-like protein) - for most inflammasomes
- NEK7 - specifically for NLRP3 inflammasome
- NAIP - specifically for NLRC4 inflammasome
- Pro-caspase-1 - for canonical inflammasomes

Note: Gasdermin D and processed cytokines (IL-1beta, IL-18) are downstream effectors/substrates, not core complex constituents.

content.content:
1→# Research: Common Constituents of NLR (Nucleotide-Binding Leucine-Rich Repeat Receptor) Complexes
     2→
     3→## Summary
     4→
     5→NLR (nucleotide-binding leucine-rich repeat) receptors are intracellular immune receptors found in both plants and animals that recognize pathogen-derived molecules and danger signals. Upon activation, they form large oligomeric signaling complexes. While the core NLR proteins share common domain architectures, the accessory proteins that physically associate with them differ significantly between plant resistosomes and animal inflammasomes.
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## PLANT NLR COMPLEX CONSTITUENTS
    10→
    11→### 1. CC-NLR Resistosomes (e.g., ZAR1)
    12→
    13→CC-NLRs (coiled-coil NLRs) form pentameric resistosome complexes that function directly as calcium-permeable cation channels at the plasma membrane.
    14→
    15→#### Core constituents:
    16→- **Sensor NLR protein** (e.g., ZAR1) - forms the pentameric resistosome
    17→- **RLCK (Receptor-Like Cytoplasmic Kinase) pseudokinases** - associates with some CC-NLRs as guardees (e.g., PBL2, RKS1 with ZAR1)
    18→
    19→### 2. Helper NLR Resistosomes (NRC, NRG1, ADR1)
    20→
    21→Helper NLRs function downstream of sensor NLRs and also form oligomeric complexes that act as calcium channels.
    22→
    23→#### Core constituents:
    24→- **Helper NLR protein** (NRG1 or ADR1 family members, or NRC proteins)
    25→- **Hexameric or pentameric oligomer** of helper NLR forms the resistosome
    26→
    27→### 3. TIR-NLR (TNL) Signaling Complexes
    28→
    29→TNLs form tetrameric resistosomes with NADase enzymatic activity. They signal through lipase-like adaptor proteins.
    30→
    31→#### Core constituents:
    32→- **TIR-NLR protein** - forms tetrameric resistosome with NADase activity
    33→- **EDS1 (Enhanced Disease Susceptibility 1)** - lipase-like signaling adaptor
    34→- **PAD4 (Phytoalexin Deficient 4)** - EDS1 heterodimer partner, lipase-like protein
    35→- **SAG101 (Senescence-Associated Gene 101)** - EDS1 heterodimer partner, lipase-like protein
    36→- EDS1-PAD4 heterodimer functions with ADR1 helper NLRs
    37→- EDS1-SAG101 heterodimer functions with NRG1 helper NLRs
    38→
    39→---
    40→
    41→## ANIMAL NLR COMPLEX CONSTITUENTS (INFLAMMASOMES)
    42→
    43→### 1. NLRP3 Inflammasome
    44→
    45→The most extensively studied inflammasome, activated by diverse stimuli.
    46→
    47→#### Core constituents:
    48→- **NLRP3 sensor** - NLR protein that oligomerizes upon activation
    49→- **NEK7 (NIMA-related kinase 7)** - bridges adjacent NLRP3 subunits; essential for NLRP3 oligomerization (kinase activity dispensable)
    50→- **ASC (Apoptosis-associated Speck-like protein containing a CARD)** - adaptor protein; contains PYD and CARD domains; forms filaments
    51→- **Caspase-1 (pro-form)** - effector cysteine protease; recruited via CARD-CARD interactions
    52→
    53→### 2. NAIP/NLRC4 Inflammasome
    54→
    55→Activated by bacterial flagellin and type III secretion system components.
    56→
    57→#### Core constituents:
    58→- **NAIP (NLR family Apoptosis Inhibitory Protein)** - sensor that directly binds bacterial ligands
    59→  - NAIP5/NAIP6 recognize flagellin (mouse)
    60→  - NAIP1/NAIP2 recognize T3SS needle/rod proteins (mouse)
    61→  - Human NAIP recognizes T3SS needle protein
    62→- **NLRC4** - adaptor NLR; contains CARD; oligomerizes with NAIP
    63→- **ASC** - adaptor protein (enhances signaling but not essential for pyroptosis)
    64→- **Caspase-1** - effector protease; can be recruited directly by NLRC4 CARD or via ASC
    65→
    66→### 3. Other Inflammasomes (NLRP1, AIM2, Pyrin)
    67→
    68→- **ASC** - required adaptor for PYD-containing sensors (AIM2, NLRP3, Pyrin)
    69→- **Caspase-1** - effector for all canonical inflammasomes
    70→- NLRP1 and NLRC4 can recruit caspase-1 directly via their CARD domains
    71→
    72→### Downstream effectors (physically associated via caspase-1 cleavage):
    73→- **Gasdermin D** - cleaved by caspase-1; N-terminal fragment forms membrane pores causing pyroptosis
    74→
    75→---
    76→
    77→## COMMON/GENERAL FEATURES ACROSS KINGDOMS
    78→
    79→1. **Oligomerization** - Both plant resistosomes and animal inflammasomes form oligomeric structures upon activation
    80→2. **Calcium signaling** - Plant resistosomes function as Ca2+ channels; animal inflammasomes also trigger Ca2+ signaling
    81→3. **Adaptor proteins** - Both systems use adaptor proteins (ASC in animals; EDS1/PAD4/SAG101 in plants for TNLs)
    82→4. **Helper/sensor relationships** - Both systems can involve sensor-helper NLR relationships
    83→
    84→---
    85→
    86→## VALIDATED REFERENCES
    87→
    88→### PMID:37602488 "NLR immune receptors: structure and function in plant disease resistance."
    89→
    90→Förderer A, Kourelis J. Biochem Soc Trans. 2023 Aug 31;51(4):1473-1483.
    91→
    92→Review covering plant NLR domain architecture and resistosome formation.
    93→
    94→* SUPPORT: PMID:37602488 "Nucleotide-binding and leucine-rich repeat receptors (NLRs) are a diverse family of intracellular immune receptors that play crucial roles in recognizing and responding to pathogen invasion in plants."
    95→    - NLRs are intracellular immune receptors in plants
    96→    - Reviews N-terminal executioner domains, NOD module, and LRR domain
    97→
    98→---
    99→
   100→### PMID:33984278 "The ZAR1 resistosome is a calcium-permeable channel triggering plant immune signaling."
   101→
   102→Bi G, Su M, Li N, et al. Cell. 2021 Jun 24;184(13):3528-3541.e12.
   103→
   104→Landmark paper demonstrating ZAR1 resistosome functions as a calcium channel.
   105→
   106→* SUPPORT: PMID:33984278 "Upon activation, the Arabidopsis NLR protein ZAR1 forms a pentameric resistosome in vitro and triggers immune responses and cell death in plants."
   107→    - ZAR1 forms pentameric resistosome
   108→    - Functions as calcium-permeable cation channel
   109→    - Glu11 residue essential for channel activity
   110→
   111→---
   112→
   113→### PMID:34140391 "Plant 'helper' immune receptors are Ca(2+)-permeable nonselective cation channels."
   114→
   115→Jacob P, Kim NH, Wu F, et al. Science. 2021 Jul 23;373(6553):420-425.
   116→
   117→Demonstrates helper NLRs (NRG1, ADR1) function as calcium channels.
   118→
   119→* SUPPORT: PMID:34140391 "Active NRG1.1 oligomerized, was enriched in plasma membrane puncta, and conferred cytoplasmic calcium ion (Ca2+) influx in plant and human cells."
   120→    - NRG1.1 and ADR1 are helper NLRs
   121→    - Both form Ca2+-permeable cation channels
   122→    - Conserved negatively charged N-terminal residues required for Ca2+ influx
   123→
   124→---
   125→
   126→### PMID:31311833 "A Coevolved EDS1-SAG101-NRG1 Module Mediates Cell Death Signaling by TIR-Domain Immune Receptors."
   127→
   128→Lapin D, Kovacova V, Sun X, et al. Plant Cell. 2019 Oct;31(10):2430-2455.
   129→
   130→Establishes the EDS1-SAG101-NRG1 signaling module in TNL immunity.
   131→
   132→* SUPPORT: PMID:31311833 "Toll-interleukin 1 receptor domain NLRs (TNLs) converge on the ENHANCED DISEASE SUSCEPTIBILITY1 (EDS1) family of lipase-like proteins for all resistance outputs."
   133→    - EDS1-SAG101-NRG1 is a coevolved cell death-signaling module
   134→    - EDS1-PAD4 and EDS1-SAG101 are distinct signaling branches
   135→    - EDS1 family proteins are lipase-like adaptors
   136→
   137→---
   138→
   139→### PMID:25621709 "The NAIP/NLRC4 inflammasomes."
   140→
   141→Vance RE. Curr Opin Immunol. 2015 Feb;32:84-9.
   142→
   143→Review of NAIP/NLRC4 inflammasome structure and function.
   144→
   145→* SUPPORT: PMID:25621709 "NAIP proteins function as specific cytosolic receptors for a variety of bacterial protein ligands. Mouse NAIP5 recognizes flagellin, the primary protein constituent of the bacterial flagellum. Mouse NAIP1 and NAIP2 detect needle and inner rod proteins, respectively, which are key structural components of a bacterial secretion system called the type III secretion system (T3SS)."
   146→    - NAIPs are sensors for bacterial ligands
   147→    - NLRC4 is the adaptor that recruits caspase-1
   148→    - NLRC4 contains a CARD domain for direct caspase-1 recruitment
   149→
   150→* SUPPORT: PMID:25621709 "NAIPs and NLRC4 are homologous to each other (~20% amino acid identity) and are members of the functionally diverse NLR superfamily"
   151→    - NAIP and NLRC4 co-oligomerize to form inflammasome
   152→    - ASC not strictly required but enhances signaling
   153→
   154→---
   155→
   156→### PMID:31189953 "Structural mechanism for NEK7-licensed activation of NLRP3 inflammasome."
   157→
   158→Sharif H, Wang L, Wang WL, et al. Nature. 2019 Jun;570(7761):338-343.
   159→
   160→Cryo-EM structure of NLRP3-NEK7 complex revealing NEK7's bridging role.
   161→
   162→* SUPPORT: PMID:31189953 "The mitotic kinase NEK7 licenses the assembly and activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome in interphase. Here we report a cryo-electron microscopy structure of inactive human NLRP3 in complex with NEK7, at a resolution of 3.8 Å."
   163→    - NEK7 bridges adjacent NLRP3 subunits
   164→    - NEK7 C-terminal lobe interacts with NLRP3 LRR and NACHT domains
   165→    - Bipartite interactions mediate NLRP3 activation
   166→
   167→* SUPPORT: PMID:31189953 "Modelling of an active NLRP3-NEK7 conformation based on the NLRC4 inflammasome predicts an additional contact between an NLRP3-bound NEK7 and a neighbouring NLRP3."
   168→    - NEK7 is essential for NLRP3 oligomerization
   169→    - NEK7 bridges gap between adjacent NLRP3 subunits in the inflammasome ring
   170→
   171→---
   172→
   173→### PMID:26814970 "NEK7 is an essential mediator of NLRP3 activation downstream of potassium efflux."
   174→
   175→He Y, Zeng MY, Yang D, et al. Nature. 2016 Feb 18;530(7590):354-7.
   176→
   177→Identification of NEK7 as essential NLRP3 inflammasome component.
   178→
   179→* SUPPORT: PMID:26814970 "Here we report the identification of NEK7, a member of the family of mammalian NIMA-related kinases (NEK proteins), as an NLRP3-binding protein that acts downstream of potassium efflux to regulate NLRP3 oligomerization and activation."
   180→    - NEK7 is essential for NLRP3 inflammasome activation
   181→    - Catalytic activity of NEK7 is dispensable
   182→    - NEK7 mediates NLRP3 oligomerization
   183→
   184→---
   185→
   186→### PMID:27329339 "ASC filament formation serves as a signal amplification mechanism for inflammasomes."
   187→
   188→Dick MS, Sborgi L, Rühl S, et al. Nat Commun. 2016 Jun 22;7:11929.
   189→
   190→Mechanistic study of ASC speck formation and its role in inflammasome signaling.
   191→
   192→* SUPPORT: PMID:27329339 "A hallmark of inflammasome activation is the ASC speck, a micrometre-sized structure formed by the inflammasome adaptor protein ASC (apoptosis-associated speck-like protein containing a CARD), which consists of a pyrin domain (PYD) and a caspase recruitment domain (CARD)."
   193→    - ASC is the central inflammasome adaptor protein
   194→    - ASC contains PYD and CARD domains
   195→    - ASC forms filaments via PYD-PYD interactions
   196→    - ASC CARD cross-links filaments into speck structure
   197→    - ASC speck serves as signal amplification mechanism
   198→
   199→* SUPPORT: PMID:27329339 "Here we show that assembly of the ASC speck involves oligomerization of ASC(PYD) into filaments and cross-linking of these filaments by ASC(CARD)."
   200→    - ASC PYD forms the filament backbone
   201→    - ASC CARD mediates filament cross-linking and caspase-1 recruitment
   202→
   203→---
   204→
   205→### PMID:27383986 "Inflammasome-activated gasdermin D causes pyroptosis by forming membrane pores."
   206→
   207→Liu X, Zhang Z, Ruan J, et al. Nature. 2016 Jul 7;535(7610):153-8.
   208→
   209→Discovery that gasdermin D forms membrane pores downstream of inflammasome activation.
   210→
   211→* SUPPORT: PMID:27383986 "Inflammatory caspases (caspases 1, 4, 5 and 11) are activated in response to microbial infection and danger signals. When activated, they cleave mouse and human gasdermin D (GSDMD) after Asp276 and Asp275, respectively, to generate an N-terminal cleavage product (GSDMD-NT) that triggers inflammatory death (pyroptosis)"
   212→    - Gasdermin D is cleaved by caspase-1
   213→    - N-terminal fragment forms membrane pores
   214→    - This is downstream effector, not a core complex constituent
   215→
   216→---
   217→
   218→## SUMMARY TABLE: Core Physical Constituents of NLR Complexes
   219→
   220→| Kingdom | Complex Type | Core Physical Constituents |
   221→|---------|--------------|---------------------------|
   222→| Plants | CC-NLR resistosome | Sensor NLR (pentamer), associated guardee/decoy proteins |
   223→| Plants | Helper NLR resistosome | Helper NLR (NRG1/ADR1/NRC; pentamer or hexamer) |
   224→| Plants | TNL resistosome + signaling | TIR-NLR (tetramer), EDS1, PAD4 or SAG101 |
   225→| Animals | NLRP3 inflammasome | NLRP3, NEK7, ASC, pro-caspase-1 |
   226→| Animals | NAIP/NLRC4 inflammasome | NAIP, NLRC4, (ASC optional), pro-caspase-1 |
   227→| Animals | AIM2/Pyrin inflammasome | Sensor (AIM2/Pyrin), ASC, pro-caspase-1 |
   228→
   229→---
   230→
   231→## Notes for GO Ontology Definition
   232→
   233→For an NLR complex definition, the following proteins should be considered as common constituents:
   234→
   235→**In plants:**
   236→- The NLR protein itself (sensor or helper)
   237→- For TNL signaling: EDS1 family lipase-like proteins (EDS1, PAD4, SAG101)
   238→- Helper NLRs (NRG1, ADR1, NRC family) for sensor NLRs that require them
   239→
   240→**In animals:**
   241→- The NLR sensor protein
   242→- ASC (apoptosis-associated speck-like protein) - for most inflammasomes
   243→- NEK7 - specifically for NLRP3 inflammasome
   244→- NAIP - specifically for NLRC4 inflammasome
   245→- Pro-caspase-1 - for canonical inflammasomes
   246→
   247→Note: Gasdermin D and processed cytokines (IL-1beta, IL-18) are downstream effectors/substrates, not core complex constituents.
   248→

60→PMID: 25621709 [Indexed for MEDLINE]
    61→
    62→Nucleotide binding domain and leucine rich repeat containing proteins (NLRs) are a functionally diverse protein family that includes approximately 20 members in both mouse and humans. The NLR family of apoptosis inhibitory proteins (NAIPs) are one particularly well-characterized NLR sub-family. In the mouse, NAIPs are encoded within a small cluster of genes that vary significantly in number and nucleotide sequence among different inbred strains. Classic genetic studies published by the Dietrich and Gros groups in 2003 were the first to demonstrate the critical role of NAIPs in host defense against bacterial infection [1,2]. The Dietrich and Gros groups showed that one particular NAIP protein, encoded by the Naip5 gene of the C57BL/6 mouse strain, functions to severely restrict intracellular replication of Legionella pneumophila, a bacterial pathogen that causes a severe pneumonia called Legionnaires’ Disease. At the time, the mechanism by which NAIP5 restricts L. pneumophila replication was entirely unclear, as was the relevance of NAIPs for defense against other bacterial pathogens or human innate immunity. In the ensuing decade, however, work from many labs has revealed the critical role of NAIPs in innate immunity in both mice and humans. There is now clear evidence that NAIPs function as cytosolic innate immune receptors for specific bacterial protein ligands. Upon detection of these ligands, NAIPs co-assemble with a downstream protein called NLRC4 to form a multiprotein complex called an inflammasome. Inflammasomes, in turn, function as platforms that activate inflammatory caspase proteases. In this review, I highlight several recent advances in our understanding of the NAIP/NLRC4 inflammasomes, with a particular emphasis on how NAIP/NLRC4 can provide beneficial functions in host defense, and conversely, how inappropriate NAIP/NLRC4 activation can lead to inflammatory disease.
    63→
    64→NAIP proteins function as specific cytosolic receptors for a variety of bacterial protein ligands. Mouse NAIP5 recognizes flagellin [3-7], the primary protein constituent of the bacterial flagellum. Mouse NAIP1 and NAIP2 detect needle and inner rod proteins, respectively, which are key structural components of a bacterial secretion system called the type III secretion system (T3SS) [3,7-10] (Figure 1). Importantly, the single human NAIP protein that has been characterized appears only to recognize the T3SS needle protein and does not appear to respond to flagellin [10]. T3SSs are evolutionarily and structurally related to flagella, and are used by many gram negative bacterial pathogens to inject ‘effector’ proteins into the host cell cytosol. T3SS effector proteins vary significantly among bacteria but are typically enzymes that manipulate host cell biology to benefit the pathogen. Given the variability of T3SS effectors, innate immune detection of the more highly conserved needle or inner rod components may represent a strategy by which the NAIPs can respond to a wide range of bacterial pathogens. However, since needle, rod and flagellin proteins comprise structures on the bacterial surface, they are not likely to be abundantly secreted. Thus, NAIPs must be highly sensitive to the presumably low levels of their ligands that are apparently inadvertently released into the host cell cytosol.
    65→
    66→Upon detection of their cognate ligands, NAIPs co-oligomerize with a downstream adaptor protein called NLRC4. Confusingly, many papers continue to refer to NLRC4 as a “sensor”, despite a lack of evidence that NLRC4 binds or senses microbial ligands at all. Instead, NLRC4 is responsible for the recruitment and activation of the Caspase-1 (CASP1) protease, downstream of NAIP activation [11,12]. CASP1 orchestrates innate anti-bacterial responses by inducing a rapid lytic cell death, called pyroptosis, that is believed to expel bacteria from their protected intracellular niche [13,14]. In addition, CASP1 also mediates the processing and release of the pro-inflammatory interleukins-1β and -18. A major innate function of IL-1β is to stimulate the recruitment of neutrophils that engulf and kill extracellular bacteria. Thus, two major effector functions of CASP1 — pyroptosis and neutrophil recruitment — appear to function synergistically to eliminate bacteria [14].
    67→
    68→NAIPs and NLRC4 are homologous to each other (~20% amino acid identity) and are members of the functionally diverse NLR superfamily, so-named because they contain a Nucleotide-binding domain (NBD) and a Leucine-rich Repeat (LRR) domain. (Despite continued confusion, NLR is not an abbreviation for Nod-like receptor [15]; indeed many NLRs are likely not receptors at all [16]). Many NLRs, including NAIPs, NLRC4, NLRP3 and NLRP1, assemble into multi-protein complexes called ‘inflammasomes’ that recruit and activate CASP1 [16,17]. Importantly, however, not all NLRs form inflammasomes; thus, the possibility of caspase-independent functions for NAIP/NLRC4 should always be considered. In addition to the NBD and LRR domains, all NLRs contain three helical domains associated with the NBD: Helical Domain 1 (HD1), Winged Helix Domain (WHD), and Helical Domain 2 (HD2) (Figure 2). Together, the NBD-HD1-WHD-HD2 module is sometimes referred to as a NOD or NACHT domain. NAIPs are distinguished from other NLRs by the presence of three (misleadingly named) Baculovirus Inhibitor-of-apoptosis Repeats (BIRs) at the N-terminus of the protein. The function of the BIRs in NAIPs remains unknown, but they are not believed to play any role in inhibition of apoptosis [18]. NLRC4 lacks BIRs, but contains an N-terminal Caspase Activation and Recruitment Domain (CARD) that can directly recruit and activate CASP1, or can indirectly recruit CASP1 via a CARD-containing adaptor protein called ASC.
    69→

But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

content.text:
All references are now validated. Let me provide a summary of the completed research.

## Summary

I have completed the research on common constituents of NLR (nucleotide-binding leucine-rich repeat receptor) complexes and created a comprehensive RESEARCH.md file at `/__w/go-ontology/go-ontology/RESEARCH.md`.

### Key Findings

**In Plants (Resistosomes):**

1. **CC-NLR Resistosomes** (e.g., ZAR1):
   - Sensor NLR protein forms pentameric resistosome
   - Functions directly as calcium-permeable cation channel (PMID:33984278)

2. **Helper NLR Resistosomes** (NRG1, ADR1, NRC family):
   - Helper NLRs form oligomeric complexes (pentamers or hexamers)
   - Also function as Ca2+-permeable cation channels (PMID:34140391)

3. **TIR-NLR (TNL) Signaling Complexes**:
   - TIR-NLR forms tetrameric resistosome with NADase activity
   - EDS1 (lipase-like adaptor protein)
   - PAD4 (forms heterodimer with EDS1, works with ADR1)
   - SAG101 (forms heterodimer with EDS1, works with NRG1) (PMID:31311833)

**In Animals (Inflammasomes):**

1. **NLRP3 Inflammasome**:
   - NLRP3 sensor protein
   - NEK7 (bridges adjacent NLRP3 subunits; kinase activity dispensable) (PMID:26814970, PMID:31189953)
   - ASC (adaptor with PYD and CARD domains; forms filaments) (PMID:27329339)
   - Pro-caspase-1 (effector protease)

2. **NAIP/NLRC4 Inflammasome**:
   - NAIP (sensor for bacterial flagellin/T3SS components) (PMID:25621709)
   - NLRC4 (adaptor NLR with CARD domain)
   - ASC (optional but enhances signaling)
   - Pro-caspase-1

3. **Other Inflammasomes** (AIM2, Pyrin, NLRP1):
   - Sensor protein
   - ASC (required for PYD-containing sensors)
   - Pro-caspase-1

### Validated References

All 12 SUPPORT text excerpts have been validated against the original publications:
- PMID:37602488 - Plant NLR review
- PMID:33984278 - ZAR1 resistosome calcium channel
- PMID:34140391 - Helper NLRs as calcium channels
- PMID:31311833 - EDS1-SAG101-NRG1 module
- PMID:25621709 - NAIP/NLRC4 inflammasome review
- PMID:31189953 - NEK7-NLRP3 structure
- PMID:26814970 - NEK7 as essential NLRP3 mediator
- PMID:27329339 - ASC filament formation
- PMID:27383986 - Gasdermin D pore formation

The RESEARCH.md file is ready for use in updating GO ontology definitions related to NLR complexes.

content.content:
1→# Research: Common Constituents of NLR (Nucleotide-Binding Leucine-Rich Repeat Receptor) Complexes
     2→
     3→## Summary
     4→
     5→NLR (nucleotide-binding leucine-rich repeat) receptors are intracellular immune receptors found in both plants and animals that recognize pathogen-derived molecules and danger signals. Upon activation, they form large oligomeric signaling complexes. While the core NLR proteins share common domain architectures, the accessory proteins that physically associate with them differ significantly between plant resistosomes and animal inflammasomes.
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## PLANT NLR COMPLEX CONSTITUENTS
    10→
    11→### 1. CC-NLR Resistosomes (e.g., ZAR1)
    12→
    13→CC-NLRs (coiled-coil NLRs) form pentameric resistosome complexes that function directly as calcium-permeable cation channels at the plasma membrane.
    14→
    15→#### Core constituents:
    16→- **Sensor NLR protein** (e.g., ZAR1) - forms the pentameric resistosome
    17→- **RLCK (Receptor-Like Cytoplasmic Kinase) pseudokinases** - associates with some CC-NLRs as guardees (e.g., PBL2, RKS1 with ZAR1)
    18→
    19→### 2. Helper NLR Resistosomes (NRC, NRG1, ADR1)
    20→
    21→Helper NLRs function downstream of sensor NLRs and also form oligomeric complexes that act as calcium channels.
    22→
    23→#### Core constituents:
    24→- **Helper NLR protein** (NRG1 or ADR1 family members, or NRC proteins)
    25→- **Hexameric or pentameric oligomer** of helper NLR forms the resistosome
    26→
    27→### 3. TIR-NLR (TNL) Signaling Complexes
    28→
    29→TNLs form tetrameric resistosomes with NADase enzymatic activity. They signal through lipase-like adaptor proteins.
    30→
    31→#### Core constituents:
    32→- **TIR-NLR protein** - forms tetrameric resistosome with NADase activity
    33→- **EDS1 (Enhanced Disease Susceptibility 1)** - lipase-like signaling adaptor
    34→- **PAD4 (Phytoalexin Deficient 4)** - EDS1 heterodimer partner, lipase-like protein
    35→- **SAG101 (Senescence-Associated Gene 101)** - EDS1 heterodimer partner, lipase-like protein
    36→- EDS1-PAD4 heterodimer functions with ADR1 helper NLRs
    37→- EDS1-SAG101 heterodimer functions with NRG1 helper NLRs
    38→
    39→---
    40→
    41→## ANIMAL NLR COMPLEX CONSTITUENTS (INFLAMMASOMES)
    42→
    43→### 1. NLRP3 Inflammasome
    44→
    45→The most extensively studied inflammasome, activated by diverse stimuli.
    46→
    47→#### Core constituents:
    48→- **NLRP3 sensor** - NLR protein that oligomerizes upon activation
    49→- **NEK7 (NIMA-related kinase 7)** - bridges adjacent NLRP3 subunits; essential for NLRP3 oligomerization (kinase activity dispensable)
    50→- **ASC (Apoptosis-associated Speck-like protein containing a CARD)** - adaptor protein; contains PYD and CARD domains; forms filaments
    51→- **Caspase-1 (pro-form)** - effector cysteine protease; recruited via CARD-CARD interactions
    52→
    53→### 2. NAIP/NLRC4 Inflammasome
    54→

file.content:
# Research: Common Constituents of NLR (Nucleotide-Binding Leucine-Rich Repeat Receptor) Complexes

## Summary

NLR (nucleotide-binding leucine-rich repeat) receptors are intracellular immune receptors found in both plants and animals that recognize pathogen-derived molecules and danger signals. Upon activation, they form large oligomeric signaling complexes. While the core NLR proteins share common domain architectures, the accessory proteins that physically associate with them differ significantly between plant resistosomes and animal inflammasomes.

---

## PLANT NLR COMPLEX CONSTITUENTS

### 1. CC-NLR Resistosomes (e.g., ZAR1)

CC-NLRs (coiled-coil NLRs) form pentameric resistosome complexes that function directly as calcium-permeable cation channels at the plasma membrane.

#### Core constituents:
- **Sensor NLR protein** (e.g., ZAR1) - forms the pentameric resistosome
- **RLCK (Receptor-Like Cytoplasmic Kinase) pseudokinases** - associates with some CC-NLRs as guardees (e.g., PBL2, RKS1 with ZAR1)

### 2. Helper NLR Resistosomes (NRC, NRG1, ADR1)

Helper NLRs function downstream of sensor NLRs and also form oligomeric complexes that act as calcium channels.

#### Core constituents:
- **Helper NLR protein** (NRG1 or ADR1 family members, or NRC proteins)
- **Hexameric or pentameric oligomer** of helper NLR forms the resistosome

### 3. TIR-NLR (TNL) Signaling Complexes

TNLs form tetrameric resistosomes with NADase enzymatic activity. They signal through lipase-like adaptor proteins.

#### Core constituents:
- **TIR-NLR protein** - forms tetrameric resistosome with NADase activity
- **EDS1 (Enhanced Disease Susceptibility 1)** - lipase-like signaling adaptor
- **PAD4 (Phytoalexin Deficient 4)** - EDS1 heterodimer partner, lipase-like protein
- **SAG101 (Senescence-Associated Gene 101)** - EDS1 heterodimer partner, lipase-like protein
- EDS1-PAD4 heterodimer functions with ADR1 helper NLRs
- EDS1-SAG101 heterodimer functions with NRG1 helper NLRs

---

## ANIMAL NLR COMPLEX CONSTITUENTS (INFLAMMASOMES)

### 1. NLRP3 Inflammasome

The most extensively studied inflammasome, activated by diverse stimuli.

#### Core constituents:
- **NLRP3 sensor** - NLR protein that oligomerizes upon activation
- **NEK7 (NIMA-related kinase 7)** - bridges adjacent NLRP3 subunits; essential for NLRP3 oligomerization (kinase activity dispensable)
- **ASC (Apoptosis-associated Speck-like protein containing a CARD)** - adaptor protein; contains PYD and CARD domains; forms filaments
- **Caspase-1 (pro-form)** - effector cysteine protease; recruited via CARD-CARD interactions

### 2. NAIP/NLRC4 Inflammasome

Activated by bacterial flagellin and type III secretion system components.

#### Core constituents:
- **NAIP (NLR family Apoptosis Inhibitory Protein)** - sensor that directly binds bacterial ligands
  - NAIP5/NAIP6 recognize flagellin (mouse)
  - NAIP1/NAIP2 recognize T3SS needle/rod proteins (mouse)
  - Human NAIP recognizes T3SS needle protein
- **NLRC4** - adaptor NLR; contains CARD; oligomerizes with NAIP
- **ASC** - adaptor protein (enhances signaling but not essential for pyroptosis)
- **Caspase-1** - effector protease; can be recruited directly by NLRC4 CARD or via ASC

### 3. Other Inflammasomes (NLRP1, AIM2, Pyrin)

- **ASC** - required adaptor for PYD-containing sensors (AIM2, NLRP3, Pyrin)
- **Caspase-1** - effector for all canonical inflammasomes
- NLRP1 and NLRC4 can recruit caspase-1 directly via their CARD domains

### Downstream effectors (physically associated via caspase-1 cleavage):
- **Gasdermin D** - cleaved by caspase-1; N-terminal fragment forms membrane pores causing pyroptosis

---

## COMMON/GENERAL FEATURES ACROSS KINGDOMS

1. **Oligomerization** - Both plant resistosomes and animal inflammasomes form oligomeric structures upon activation
2. **Calcium signaling** - Plant resistosomes function as Ca2+ channels; animal inflammasomes also trigger Ca2+ signaling
3. **Adaptor proteins** - Both systems use adaptor proteins (ASC in animals; EDS1/PAD4/SAG101 in plants for TNLs)
4. **Helper/sensor relationships** - Both systems can involve sensor-helper NLR relationships

---

## VALIDATED REFERENCES

### PMID:37602488 "NLR immune receptors: structure and function in plant disease resistance."

Förderer A, Kourelis J. Biochem Soc Trans. 2023 Aug 31;51(4):1473-1483.

Review covering plant NLR domain architecture and resistosome formation.

* SUPPORT: PMID:37602488 "Nucleotide-binding and leucine-rich repeat receptors (NLRs) are a diverse family of intracellular immune receptors that play crucial roles in recognizing and responding to pathogen invasion in plants."
    - NLRs are intracellular immune receptors in plants
    - Reviews N-terminal executioner domains, NOD module, and LRR domain

---

### PMID:33984278 "The ZAR1 resistosome is a calcium-permeable channel triggering plant immune signaling."

Bi G, Su M, Li N, et al. Cell. 2021 Jun 24;184(13):3528-3541.e12.

Landmark paper demonstrating ZAR1 resistosome functions as a calcium channel.

* SUPPORT: PMID:33984278 "Upon activation, the Arabidopsis NLR protein ZAR1 forms a pentameric resistosome in vitro and triggers immune responses and cell death in plants."
    - ZAR1 forms pentameric resistosome
    - Functions as calcium-permeable cation channel
    - Glu11 residue essential for channel activity

---

### PMID:34140391 "Plant 'helper' immune receptors are Ca(2+)-permeable nonselective cation channels."

Jacob P, Kim NH, Wu F, et al. Science. 2021 Jul 23;373(6553):420-425.

Demonstrates helper NLRs (NRG1, ADR1) function as calcium channels.

* SUPPORT: PMID:34140391 "Active NRG1.1 oligomerized, was enriched in plasma membrane puncta, and conferred cytoplasmic calcium ion (Ca2+) influx in plant and human cells."
    - NRG1.1 and ADR1 are helper NLRs
    - Both form Ca2+-permeable cation channels
    - Conserved negatively charged N-terminal residues required for Ca2+ influx

---

### PMID:31311833 "A Coevolved EDS1-SAG101-NRG1 Module Mediates Cell Death Signaling by TIR-Domain Immune Receptors."

Lapin D, Kovacova V, Sun X, et al. Plant Cell. 2019 Oct;31(10):2430-2455.

Establishes the EDS1-SAG101-NRG1 signaling module in TNL immunity.

* SUPPORT: PMID:31311833 "Toll-interleukin 1 receptor domain NLRs (TNLs) converge on the ENHANCED DISEASE SUSCEPTIBILITY1 (EDS1) family of lipase-like proteins for all resistance outputs."
    - EDS1-SAG101-NRG1 is a coevolved cell death-signaling module
    - EDS1-PAD4 and EDS1-SAG101 are distinct signaling branches
    - EDS1 family proteins are lipase-like adaptors

---

### PMID:25621709 "The NAIP/NLRC4 inflammasomes."

Vance RE. Curr Opin Immunol. 2015 Feb;32:84-9.

Review of NAIP/NLRC4 inflammasome structure and function.

* SUPPORT: PMID:25621709 "There is now clear evidence that NAIPs function as cytosolic innate immune receptors for specific bacterial protein ligands."
    - NAIPs are sensors for bacterial ligands
    - NLRC4 is the adaptor that recruits caspase-1
    - NLRC4 contains a CARD domain for direct caspase-1 recruitment

* SUPPORT: PMID:25621709 "NAIPs and NLRC4 are homologous to each other (~20% amino acid identity) and are members of the functionally diverse NLR superfamily"
    - NAIP and NLRC4 co-oligomerize to form inflammasome
    - ASC not strictly required but enhances signaling

---

### PMID:31189953 "Structural mechanism for NEK7-licensed activation of NLRP3 inflammasome."

Sharif H, Wang L, Wang WL, et al. Nature. 2019 Jun;570(7761):338-343.

Cryo-EM structure of NLRP3-NEK7 complex revealing NEK7's bridging role.

* SUPPORT: PMID:31189953 "The mitotic kinase NEK7 licenses the assembly and activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome in interphase. Here we report a cryo-electron microscopy structure of inactive human NLRP3 in complex with NEK7, at a resolution of 3.8 Å."
    - NEK7 bridges adjacent NLRP3 subunits
    - NEK7 C-terminal lobe interacts with NLRP3 LRR and NACHT domains
    - Bipartite interactions mediate NLRP3 activation

* SUPPORT: PMID:31189953 "Modelling of an active NLRP3-NEK7 conformation based on the NLRC4 inflammasome predicts an additional contact between an NLRP3-bound NEK7 and a neighbouring NLRP3."
    - NEK7 is essential for NLRP3 oligomerization
    - NEK7 bridges gap between adjacent NLRP3 subunits in the inflammasome ring

---

### PMID:26814970 "NEK7 is an essential mediator of NLRP3 activation downstream of potassium efflux."

He Y, Zeng MY, Yang D, et al. Nature. 2016 Feb 18;530(7590):354-7.

Identification of NEK7 as essential NLRP3 inflammasome component.

* SUPPORT: PMID:26814970 "Here we report the identification of NEK7, a member of the family of mammalian NIMA-related kinases (NEK proteins), as an NLRP3-binding protein that acts downstream of potassium efflux to regulate NLRP3 oligomerization and activation."
    - NEK7 is essential for NLRP3 inflammasome activation
    - Catalytic activity of NEK7 is dispensable
    - NEK7 mediates NLRP3 oligomerization

---

### PMID:27329339 "ASC filament formation serves as a signal amplification mechanism for inflammasomes."

Dick MS, Sborgi L, Rühl S, et al. Nat Commun. 2016 Jun 22;7:11929.

Mechanistic study of ASC speck formation and its role in inflammasome signaling.

* SUPPORT: PMID:27329339 "A hallmark of inflammasome activation is the ASC speck, a micrometre-sized structure formed by the inflammasome adaptor protein ASC (apoptosis-associated speck-like protein containing a CARD), which consists of a pyrin domain (PYD) and a caspase recruitment domain (CARD)."
    - ASC is the central inflammasome adaptor protein
    - ASC contains PYD and CARD domains
    - ASC forms filaments via PYD-PYD interactions
    - ASC CARD cross-links filaments into speck structure
    - ASC speck serves as signal amplification mechanism

* SUPPORT: PMID:27329339 "Here we show that assembly of the ASC speck involves oligomerization of ASC(PYD) into filaments and cross-linking of these filaments by ASC(CARD)."
    - ASC PYD forms the filament backbone
    - ASC CARD mediates filament cross-linking and caspase-1 recruitment

---

### PMID:27383986 "Inflammasome-activated gasdermin D causes pyroptosis by forming membrane pores."

Liu X, Zhang Z, Ruan J, et al. Nature. 2016 Jul 7;535(7610):153-8.

Discovery that gasdermin D forms membrane pores downstream of inflammasome activation.

* SUPPORT: PMID:27383986 "Inflammatory caspases (caspases 1, 4, 5 and 11) are activated in response to microbial infection and danger signals. When activated, they cleave mouse and human gasdermin D (GSDMD) after Asp276 and Asp275, respectively, to generate an N-terminal cleavage product (GSDMD-NT) that triggers inflammatory death (pyroptosis)"
    - Gasdermin D is cleaved by caspase-1
    - N-terminal fragment forms membrane pores
    - This is downstream effector, not a core complex constituent

---

## SUMMARY TABLE: Core Physical Constituents of NLR Complexes

| Kingdom | Complex Type | Core Physical Constituents |
|---------|--------------|---------------------------|
| Plants | CC-NLR resistosome | Sensor NLR (pentamer), associated guardee/decoy proteins |
| Plants | Helper NLR resistosome | Helper NLR (NRG1/ADR1/NRC; pentamer or hexamer) |
| Plants | TNL resistosome + signaling | TIR-NLR (tetramer), EDS1, PAD4 or SAG101 |
| Animals | NLRP3 inflammasome | NLRP3, NEK7, ASC, pro-caspase-1 |
| Animals | NAIP/NLRC4 inflammasome | NAIP, NLRC4, (ASC optional), pro-caspase-1 |
| Animals | AIM2/Pyrin inflammasome | Sensor (AIM2/Pyrin), ASC, pro-caspase-1 |

---

## Notes for GO Ontology Definition

For an NLR complex definition, the following proteins should be considered as common constituents:

**In plants:**
- The NLR protein itself (sensor or helper)
- For TNL signaling: EDS1 family lipase-like proteins (EDS1, PAD4, SAG101)
- Helper NLRs (NRG1, ADR1, NRC family) for sensor NLRs that require them

**In animals:**
- The NLR sensor protein
- ASC (apoptosis-associated speck-like protein) - for most inflammasomes
- NEK7 - specifically for NLRP3 inflammasome
- NAIP - specifically for NLRC4 inflammasome
- Pro-caspase-1 - for canonical inflammasomes

Note: Gasdermin D and processed cytokines (IL-1beta, IL-18) are downstream effectors/substrates, not core complex constituents.

1→[Term]
     2→id: GO:7770053
     3→name: nucleotide-binding leucine-rich repeat receptor complex
     4→namespace: cellular_component
     5→def: "A protein complex that contains one or more nucleotide-binding leucine-rich repeat (NLR) proteins and functions as an intracellular innate immune receptor. NLR proteins share a conserved tripartite domain architecture consisting of a central nucleotide-binding domain (NB-ARC/NOD), a C-terminal leucine-rich repeat (LRR) domain, and a variable N-terminal effector domain (such as coiled-coil, TIR, CARD, or PYD domains). Upon activation by pathogen effectors or danger signals, NLR complexes oligomerize and trigger downstream immune responses, including programmed cell death. In plants, activated NLR complexes are called resistosomes; in animals, they form inflammasomes." [PMID:26355215, PMID:30948526, PMID:30948527]

def: "A protein complex that contains one or more nucleotide-binding leucine-rich repeat (NLR) proteins and functions as an intracellular innate immune receptor. NLR proteins share a conserved tripartite domain architecture consisting of a central nucleotide-binding domain (NB-ARC/NOD), a C-terminal leucine-rich repeat (LRR) domain, and a variable N-terminal effector domain (such as coiled-coil, TIR, CARD, or PYD domains). Upon activation by pathogen effectors or danger signals, NLR complexes oligomerize and trigger downstream immune responses, including programmed cell death. In plants, activated NLR complexes are called resistosomes; in animals, they form inflammasomes." [PMID:26355215, PMID:30948526, PMID:30948527]

content.thinking:
Now I need to craft a new definition that:
1. Removes domain descriptions
2. Includes common constituents

Based on the research:
- In animals: NLR + ASC adaptor protein (for PYD-containing NLRs) + caspases
- In plants: NLR + EDS1 family lipase-like proteins (for TNLs) + helper NLRs

Let me write a cleaner definition that focuses on function and common constituents rather than domain architecture.

New definition should be something like:

"A protein complex that contains one or more nucleotide-binding leucine-rich repeat (NLR) proteins and functions as an intracellular innate immune receptor. Upon activation by pathogen effectors or danger signals, NLR proteins oligomerize with various adaptor and effector proteins to trigger downstream immune responses, including programmed cell death. In animals, inflammasome complexes typically contain ASC (an adaptor protein) and caspase-1. In plants, resistosome signaling complexes may include lipase-like adaptors such as EDS1, PAD4, or SAG101, and helper NLRs."

Let me refine this a bit more to be concise and accurate.
